summary: An overview of Warren Buffett's value investing philosophy, detailing his six-step methodology for evaluating companies based on intrinsic value rather than market fluctuations. It highlights his emphasis on long-term holding, understanding the underlying business, and seeking companies with a 'protective moat'.

principles.rationale: Making prudent financial choices and increasing your knowledge in the areas you want to participate in is the best means to success.
You must weigh and analyze the business behind a stock to assess where a company is going, which requires focusing on businesses you actually understand.
The first rule of investing is to protect your capital, which means you should always be informed, do your research, and maintain your investing temperament.

principles.statement: Plan to own a stock for at least 10 years.
principles.rationale: A business needs time to evolve, and investors should seek ownership in quality companies capable of generating long-term earnings rather than short-term capital gains.
principles.source_quote: If you aren't willing to own a stock for 10 years, don't even think about owning it for 10 minutes

frameworks.name: Six-Step Investment Methodology
frameworks.when_to_apply: When evaluating a company to determine if it is a high-quality, undervalued investment.
steps: Analyze Company Performance: Check if the Return on Equity (ROE) has been consistently high over the past five to 10 years.
Evaluate Company Debt: Look for a low Debt-to-Equity (D/E) ratio, ensuring earnings growth comes from shareholders' equity rather than borrowed money.
Check Profit Margins: Ensure profit margins are high and have been consistently increasing over at least five years.
Verify Public History: Only consider companies that have been public for at least 10 years and whose mechanics you fully comprehend.
Assess Commodity Reliance: Avoid companies with indistinguishable products; look for a 'protective moat' that gives a competitive advantage.
Determine if it's Cheap: Calculate the intrinsic value based on fundamentals and compare it to the current market capitalization to see if it is undervalued.

mental_models.name: Voting Machine vs. Weighing Machine
mental_models.description: A metaphor for the stock market borrowed from Benjamin Graham. In the short term, the market fluctuates based on popularity and sentiment (voting), but in the long term, it reflects a company's true intrinsic value and ability to generate earnings (weighing).

mental_models.name: Protective Moat
mental_models.description: A competitive advantage or characteristic that is hard for competitors to replicate. The wider the moat, the tougher it is for a competitor to gain market share.
mental_models.example: Avoiding companies whose products are indistinguishable from competitors or that rely solely on a commodity like oil or gas.

heuristics: Wait for a reasonable valuation, then pounce when the market corrects.
Don't sell when your holdings falter; buy more.
Look at financial metrics (ROE, profit margins) over a 5 to 10 year historical period, not just the last year.
90/10 Rule: Allocate 90% of a portfolio to a low-cost S&P 500 index fund and 10% to short-term government bonds.

signature_quotes.text: In the short run, the market is a voting machine, but in the long run, it is a weighing machine.
signature_quotes.context: A famous paraphrase of a Benjamin Graham quote explaining why Buffett ignores short-term market intricacies.

signature_quotes.text: Just keep buying. American business is going to do fine over time, so you know the investment universe is going to do very well.
signature_quotes.context: Advice given in a CNBC interview on resisting the urge to panic and sell when holdings falter.

signature_quotes.text: If you aren't willing to own a stock for 10 years, don't even think about owning it for 10 minutes.
signature_quotes.context: From his 1996 letter to Berkshire Hathaway shareholders, emphasizing long-term holding.

anti_patterns.description: Investing in businesses or industries you do not fully understand.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: If you don't comprehend the mechanics of the business, you cannot accurately analyze its stocks or assess where the company is going in the future.

anti_patterns.description: Panicking and selling holdings when they falter.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Markets naturally correct, and quality businesses will recover over time. Selling during a downturn prevents you from capitalizing on long-term growth.

anti_patterns.description: Believing in the Efficient Market Hypothesis (EMH).
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Assuming stocks always trade at their fair value blinds investors to opportunities where quality stocks are temporarily undervalued by the market.
